  • Order, Absolute Value, and Completeness of ℝ

  • L05 Existence of √2, Density, and Intervals
  • L06 Nested Intervals and the Foundations of Sequences
  • L07 Boundedness and the Algebra of Limits
  • L08 Algebraic Facts, Limit Laws in Practice, and Monotone Convergence
  • L09 Subsequences and Their Role in Convergence and Divergence
  • L10 The Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem and Limit Superior/Inferior
  • L11 Properties of the Limit Superior
  • L12 The Cauchy Criterion

  • L014 Review: Limsup/Liminf, p-Series, Iterative Sequences, and Subsequences
  • L015 Continuous Functions on Intervals

Numerical Linear Algebra

Newton's Method for Systems, Vector Calculus, and Gauss–Newton

Newton’s Method for Systems, Vector Calculus, and Gauss–Newton

The previous chapter derived Newton’s method for a single equation and sketched its generalization to systems. This chapter makes that generalization precise, tests it against two contrasting worked examples — one where it converges beautifully, one where it doesn’t — and then builds the vector-calculus machinery needed to push the idea one step further: applying Newton’s method not just to solve equations, but to minimize a sum of squared residuals. That last destination, the Gauss–Newton method, turns out to be nothing more than the least-squares normal equations from many chapters ago, reborn in a nonlinear setting.

Linearization Idea

Suppose we want to solve F(x⃗)=0⃗F(\vec x) = \vec 0F(x)=0 for F:Rn→RnF:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^nF:Rn→Rn. Exactly as in the scalar case, linearize FFF near the current iterate x⃗k\vec x_kxk​: F(x⃗k+Δx⃗)≈F(x⃗k)+DF(x⃗k) Δx⃗.F(\vec x_k+\Delta\vec x) \approx F(\vec x_k) + DF(\vec x_k)\,\Delta\vec x.F(xk​+Δx)≈F(xk​)+DF(xk​)Δx. Forcing this linear approximation to vanish gives the Newton step: DF(x⃗k) Δx⃗=−F(x⃗k),x⃗k+1=x⃗k+Δx⃗.DF(\vec x_k)\,\Delta\vec x = -F(\vec x_k), \qquad \vec x_{k+1} = \vec x_k+\Delta\vec x.DF(xk​)Δx=−F(xk​),xk+1​=xk​+Δx.

Newton’s method for systems

Given x⃗0\vec x_0x0​, repeat:

  1. Solve DF(x⃗k) Δx⃗k=−F(x⃗k)DF(\vec x_k)\,\Delta\vec x_k = -F(\vec x_k)DF(xk​)Δxk​=−F(xk​).
  2. Update x⃗k+1=x⃗k+Δx⃗k\vec x_{k+1} = \vec x_k+\Delta\vec x_kxk+1​=xk​+Δxk​.

Both F(x⃗k)F(\vec x_k)F(xk​) and the Jacobian DF(x⃗k)DF(\vec x_k)DF(xk​) must be recomputed at every iteration.

Local Convergence Theorem

The scalar convergence theorem from the previous chapter — quadratic convergence near a simple root — has a direct multivariable analogue, with "f′(r)≠0f'(r)\neq0f′(r)=0" replaced by the natural vector condition: the Jacobian at the root must be invertible.

Newton’s method — local convergence

Let F∈C1(D)F\in C^1(D)F∈C1(D) on a bounded, closed, convex set D⊂RnD\subset\mathbb{R}^nD⊂Rn. Suppose x⃗∗∈D\vec x^*\in Dx∗∈D satisfies F(x⃗∗)=0⃗F(\vec x^*)=\vec0F(x∗)=0 and det⁡(DF(x⃗∗))≠0\det(DF(\vec x^*))\neq0det(DF(x∗))=0. Then there exists ε>0\varepsilon>0ε>0 such that whenever ∥x⃗0−x⃗∗∥<ε\|\vec x_0-\vec x^*\|<\varepsilon∥x0​−x∗∥<ε, the iterates x⃗n+1=x⃗n−[DF(x⃗n)]−1F(x⃗n)\vec x_{n+1} = \vec x_n - [DF(\vec x_n)]^{-1}F(\vec x_n)xn+1​=xn​−[DF(xn​)]−1F(xn​) are well-defined and x⃗n→x⃗∗\vec x_n \to \vec x^*xn​→x∗.

Interpretation

Newton’s method remains a fundamentally local method in this setting too: sufficiently close initial guesses near a solution with invertible Jacobian converge, but the theorem offers no guarantee otherwise. If the Jacobian at the solution is singular, this theorem simply doesn’t apply — a case worth taking seriously, as the second worked example below shows.

Worked Example: A Nonlinear 2×22\times22×2 System

Apply Newton’s method to {x2+4x−2y−1=0,x2+5y−4=0.F(x,y)=[x2+4x−2y−1x2+5y−4].\begin{cases}x^2+4x-2y-1=0,\\ x^2+5y-4=0.\end{cases} \qquad F(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix}x^2+4x-2y-1\\x^2+5y-4\end{bmatrix}.{x2+4x−2y−1=0,x2+5y−4=0.​F(x,y)=[x2+4x−2y−1x2+5y−4​].

Jacobian. DF(x,y)=[2x+4−22x5].DF(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix}2x+4 & -2\\ 2x & 5\end{bmatrix}.DF(x,y)=[2x+42x​−25​].

Checking invertibility on a region. On D=[0,2]×[0,0.8]D=[0,2]\times[0,0.8]D=[0,2]×[0,0.8], det⁡(DF(x,y))=(2x+4)(5)−(−2)(2x)=14x+20>0for all (x,y)∈D,\det(DF(x,y)) = (2x+4)(5) - (-2)(2x) = 14x+20 > 0 \quad\text{for all }(x,y)\in D,det(DF(x,y))=(2x+4)(5)−(−2)(2x)=14x+20>0for all (x,y)∈D, so the Jacobian is invertible throughout DDD — exactly the hypothesis the convergence theorem needs, and it’s worth checking before iterating rather than after, since it’s what justifies trusting the process at all.

First iteration. Start at x⃗0=(0,0)\vec x_0=(0,0)x0​=(0,0): DF(x⃗0)=[4−205],F(x⃗0)=[−1−4].DF(\vec x_0) = \begin{bmatrix}4&-2\\0&5\end{bmatrix}, \qquad F(\vec x_0) = \begin{bmatrix}-1\\-4\end{bmatrix}.DF(x0​)=[40​−25​],F(x0​)=[−1−4​]. Solving DF(x⃗0) Δx⃗0=−F(x⃗0)=[14]DF(\vec x_0)\,\Delta\vec x_0 = -F(\vec x_0) = \begin{bmatrix}1\\4\end{bmatrix}DF(x0​)Δx0​=−F(x0​)=[14​] by back-substitution (the system is already triangular): the second row gives 5Δy=4⇒Δy=0.85\Delta y=4\Rightarrow\Delta y=0.85Δy=4⇒Δy=0.8; substituting into the first gives 4Δx−1.6=1⇒Δx=0.654\Delta x - 1.6=1 \Rightarrow \Delta x=0.654Δx−1.6=1⇒Δx=0.65. So Δx⃗0=[0.650.8],x⃗1=[0.650.8].\Delta\vec x_0 = \begin{bmatrix}0.65\\0.8\end{bmatrix}, \qquad \vec x_1 = \begin{bmatrix}0.65\\0.8\end{bmatrix}.Δx0​=[0.650.8​],x1​=[0.650.8​].

Second iteration. At x⃗1=(0.65,0.8)\vec x_1=(0.65,0.8)x1​=(0.65,0.8): F(x⃗1)=[0.42250.4225],DF(x⃗1)=[5.3−21.35].F(\vec x_1) = \begin{bmatrix}0.4225\\0.4225\end{bmatrix}, \qquad DF(\vec x_1) = \begin{bmatrix}5.3&-2\\1.3&5\end{bmatrix}.F(x1​)=[0.42250.4225​],DF(x1​)=[5.31.3​−25​]. Solving gives Δx⃗1≈[−0.1016−0.0581]\Delta\vec x_1 \approx \begin{bmatrix}-0.1016\\-0.0581\end{bmatrix}Δx1​≈[−0.1016−0.0581​], so x⃗2≈[0.54840.7419].\vec x_2 \approx \begin{bmatrix}0.5484\\0.7419\end{bmatrix}.x2​≈[0.54840.7419​].

Checking against the exact solution. This system is simple enough to solve directly, which lets us confirm Newton’s method is actually converging rather than just producing plausible-looking numbers. From the second equation, y=(4−x2)/5y = (4-x^2)/5y=(4−x2)/5; substituting into the first and clearing denominators gives 7x2+20x−13=07x^2+20x-13=07x2+20x−13=0, with root in DDD at x∗=−20+76414≈0.5455,y∗=4−(x∗)25≈0.7405.x^* = \frac{-20+\sqrt{764}}{14} \approx 0.5455, \qquad y^* = \frac{4-(x^*)^2}{5}\approx 0.7405.x∗=14−20+764​​≈0.5455,y∗=54−(x∗)2​≈0.7405. The iterates x⃗1,x⃗2\vec x_1, \vec x_2x1​,x2​ are visibly closing in on x⃗∗≈(0.5455,0.7405)\vec x^*\approx(0.5455, 0.7405)x∗≈(0.5455,0.7405) — exactly the behavior the convergence theorem promised.

Example: When the Jacobian Is Singular

Not every root is this well-behaved. Consider {xy−1=0,x2+y2−2=0.F(x,y)=[xy−1x2+y2−2],DF(x,y)=[yx2x2y].\begin{cases}xy-1=0,\\ x^2+y^2-2=0.\end{cases} \qquad F(x,y)=\begin{bmatrix}xy-1\\x^2+y^2-2\end{bmatrix}, \qquad DF(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix}y&x\\2x&2y\end{bmatrix}.{xy−1=0,x2+y2−2=0.​F(x,y)=[xy−1x2+y2−2​],DF(x,y)=[y2x​x2y​].

At x⃗∗=(1,1)\vec x^* = (1,1)x∗=(1,1): F(1,1)=[00]F(1,1) = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix}F(1,1)=[00​], confirming this is a genuine root. But DF(1,1)=[1122],det⁡(DF(1,1))=0.DF(1,1) = \begin{bmatrix}1&1\\2&2\end{bmatrix}, \qquad \det(DF(1,1)) = 0.DF(1,1)=[12​12​],det(DF(1,1))=0.

Why this matters

Since the Jacobian is singular exactly at the solution, the local convergence theorem simply does not apply here. Starting exactly at (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) needs no iteration at all — but near (1,1)(1,1)(1,1), the Newton step DF(x,y)−1F(x,y)DF(x,y)^{-1}F(x,y)DF(x,y)−1F(x,y) becomes undefined or numerically unstable, since it requires inverting a matrix that is nearly singular right where accuracy matters most. The honest conclusion: Newton’s method is not guaranteed to behave well near a solution with a singular Jacobian, and in practice it often doesn’t.

Geometrically, this system describes the intersection of a hyperbola xy=1xy=1xy=1 (i.e. y=1/xy=1/xy=1/x) and a circle x2+y2=2x^2+y^2=2x2+y2=2:

The singular Jacobian corresponds exactly to the point where these two curves meet tangentially rather than crossing transversally — the hyperbola and circle graze each other at (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) instead of cutting cleanly through, and it’s precisely that tangency that makes the linearization degenerate.

Vector Calculus Review

Pushing Newton’s method further — toward minimizing a sum of squares rather than solving equations directly — requires a bit more vector-calculus vocabulary than the Jacobian alone.

Scalar-valued function

f(x1,…,xn):Rn→Rf(x_1,\dots,x_n):\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}f(x1​,…,xn​):Rn→R. Using the row-vector convention adopted here, its gradient is ∇f=[fx1⋯fxn].\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix}f_{x_1} & \cdots & f_{x_n}\end{bmatrix}.∇f=[fx1​​​⋯​fxn​​​].

Vector-valued function

F⃗(x1,…,xn)=[f1(x⃗)⋮fm(x⃗)]\vec F(x_1,\dots,x_n) = \begin{bmatrix}f_1(\vec x)\\\vdots\\f_m(\vec x)\end{bmatrix}F(x1​,…,xn​)=​f1​(x)⋮fm​(x)​​, F⃗:Rn→Rm\vec F:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^mF:Rn→Rm. Its Jacobian stacks the gradients of each component as rows: DF⃗=[∇f1⋮∇fm]=[∂f1∂x1⋯∂f1∂xn⋮⋱⋮∂fm∂x1⋯∂fm∂xn].D\vec F = \begin{bmatrix}\nabla f_1\\\vdots\\\nabla f_m\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1}&\cdots&\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n}\\\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\\dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_1}&\cdots&\dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_n}\end{bmatrix}.DF=​∇f1​⋮∇fm​​​=​∂x1​∂f1​​⋮∂x1​∂fm​​​⋯⋱⋯​∂xn​∂f1​​⋮∂xn​∂fm​​​​.

This is exactly the Jacobian introduced in the previous chapter and used throughout this one — restated here in a form (mmm possibly different from nnn) general enough to handle the residual vectors of the next section, which need not have as many components as there are unknowns.

Two product rules will be needed shortly, both direct generalizations of the familiar single-variable product rule:

Dot product rule

For u⃗,v⃗:Rn→Rm\vec u,\vec v:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^mu,v:Rn→Rm, ∇(u⃗Tv⃗)=v⃗TDu⃗+u⃗TDv⃗.\nabla(\vec u^T\vec v) = \vec v^TD\vec u + \vec u^TD\vec v.∇(uTv)=vTDu+uTDv.

Matrix–vector product rule

If A=A(x⃗)A=A(\vec x)A=A(x) is matrix-valued and v⃗=v⃗(x⃗)\vec v=\vec v(\vec x)v=v(x) is vector-valued, D(Av⃗)=A Dv⃗+∑i=1mvi Da⃗i,D(A\vec v) = A\,D\vec v + \sum_{i=1}^m v_i\,D\vec a_i,D(Av)=ADv+∑i=1m​vi​Dai​, where a⃗i\vec a_iai​ is the iii-th column of AAA.

Constant-matrix special case

If AAA doesn’t depend on x⃗\vec xx, every Da⃗i=0D\vec a_i = 0Dai​=0, and the rule collapses to the simple D(Av⃗)=A Dv⃗D(A\vec v) = A\,D\vec vD(Av)=ADv — the case that will actually apply below, when AAA turns out to be constant in the linear least-squares setting.

Nonlinear Least Squares and Gauss–Newton

Recall the linear least-squares problem from much earlier in this course: given an overdetermined Ax⃗≈b⃗A\vec x\approx\vec bAx≈b, find x⃗ˉ\bar{\vec x}xˉ minimizing ∥b⃗−Ax⃗∥2\|\vec b-A\vec x\|_2∥b−Ax∥2​, solvable via the normal equations, QR, or SVD. What if the residual isn’t linear in x⃗\vec xx at all?

Nonlinear least squares setup

Given residual functions r1(x⃗),…,rm(x⃗)r_1(\vec x),\dots,r_m(\vec x)r1​(x),…,rm​(x), collect them as r⃗(x⃗)=[r1(x⃗)⋮rm(x⃗)]\vec r(\vec x) = \begin{bmatrix}r_1(\vec x)\\\vdots\\r_m(\vec x)\end{bmatrix}r(x)=​r1​(x)⋮rm​(x)​​, and minimize E(x⃗)=12∑i=1mri(x⃗)2=12r⃗(x⃗)Tr⃗(x⃗).E(\vec x) = \frac12\sum_{i=1}^mr_i(\vec x)^2 = \frac12\vec r(\vec x)^T\vec r(\vec x).E(x)=21​∑i=1m​ri​(x)2=21​r(x)Tr(x).

First-order optimality

To minimize EEE, set its gradient to zero. Using the dot product rule with u⃗=v⃗=r⃗\vec u=\vec v=\vec ru=v=r: ∇E(x⃗)=∇(12r⃗Tr⃗)=12(r⃗TDr⃗+r⃗TDr⃗)=r⃗TDr⃗.\nabla E(\vec x) = \nabla\left(\frac12\vec r^T\vec r\right) = \frac12\left(\vec r^TD\vec r + \vec r^TD\vec r\right) = \vec r^TD\vec r.∇E(x)=∇(21​rTr)=21​(rTDr+rTDr)=rTDr. So the optimality condition is r⃗TDr⃗=0⃗\vec r^TD\vec r = \vec 0rTDr=0; transposing gives the equivalent column-vector form. Writing J(x⃗):=Dr⃗(x⃗)J(\vec x) := D\vec r(\vec x)J(x):=Dr(x), this becomes the nonlinear normal equations: J(x⃗)Tr⃗(x⃗)=0⃗.J(\vec x)^T\vec r(\vec x) = \vec0.J(x)Tr(x)=0.

The name is deliberate — this is the direct nonlinear generalization of the linear normal equations AT(Ax⃗−b⃗)=0A^T(A\vec x-\vec b)=0AT(Ax−b)=0 from the least-squares chapters, with JJJ playing the role that the constant matrix AAA played there.

Newton’s method applied to the normal equations

The nonlinear normal equations are themselves a system of equations, F(x⃗):=J(x⃗)Tr⃗(x⃗)=0⃗F(\vec x) := J(\vec x)^T\vec r(\vec x) = \vec 0F(x):=J(x)Tr(x)=0, so nothing stops us from applying Newton’s method (from earlier in this chapter) directly to them. Doing so requires DFDFDF, which a standard calculation (using the matrix–vector product rule above, since JJJ depends on x⃗\vec xx) gives as DF(x⃗)=J(x⃗)TJ(x⃗)+∑i=1mri(x⃗) ∇2ri(x⃗).DF(\vec x) = J(\vec x)^TJ(\vec x) + \sum_{i=1}^mr_i(\vec x)\,\nabla^2r_i(\vec x).DF(x)=J(x)TJ(x)+∑i=1m​ri​(x)∇2ri​(x). The exact Newton step then solves (JkTJk+∑i=1mri(x⃗k) ∇2ri(x⃗k))p⃗k=−JkTr⃗(x⃗k),Jk:=J(x⃗k).\left(J_k^TJ_k + \sum_{i=1}^mr_i(\vec x_k)\,\nabla^2r_i(\vec x_k)\right)\vec p_k = -J_k^T\vec r(\vec x_k), \qquad J_k := J(\vec x_k).(JkT​Jk​+∑i=1m​ri​(xk​)∇2ri​(xk​))p​k​=−JkT​r(xk​),Jk​:=J(xk​).

The Gauss–Newton approximation

That second-derivative term is expensive — it requires the full Hessian of every residual component. Near a good fit, though, the residuals ri(x⃗)r_i(\vec x)ri​(x) themselves are small, so the whole sum ∑iri∇2ri\sum_ir_i\nabla^2r_i∑i​ri​∇2ri​ tends to be small too (it’s a sum of small numbers times curvature terms), and dropping it is often an excellent approximation: DF(x⃗)≈J(x⃗)TJ(x⃗).DF(\vec x) \approx J(\vec x)^TJ(\vec x).DF(x)≈J(x)TJ(x).

Gauss–Newton method
  1. Form the residual r⃗(x⃗k)\vec r(\vec x_k)r(xk​).
  2. Form the Jacobian Jk=Dr⃗(x⃗k)J_k = D\vec r(\vec x_k)Jk​=Dr(xk​).
  3. Solve JkTJk p⃗k=−JkTr⃗(x⃗k)J_k^TJ_k\,\vec p_k = -J_k^T\vec r(\vec x_k)JkT​Jk​p​k​=−JkT​r(xk​).
  4. Update x⃗k+1=x⃗k+p⃗k\vec x_{k+1} = \vec x_k+\vec p_kxk+1​=xk​+p​k​.

Analogy with linear least squares

The name of this method is not a coincidence, and the connection can be made completely explicit. For a linear residual r⃗(x⃗)=Ax⃗−b⃗\vec r(\vec x) = A\vec x-\vec br(x)=Ax−b, the Jacobian is simply the constant matrix J(x⃗)=AJ(\vec x)=AJ(x)=A (exactly the special case flagged in the constant-matrix product rule above). Substituting into the Gauss–Newton step: ATA p⃗k=−AT(Ax⃗k−b⃗).A^TA\,\vec p_k = -A^T(A\vec x_k-\vec b).ATAp​k​=−AT(Axk​−b).

Gauss–Newton as a direct generalization

This is precisely the normal-equations framework from the linear least-squares chapters, rearranged as an update rule rather than a one-shot solve. Gauss–Newton is, quite literally, what the normal equations look like once the residual is allowed to curve — every step re-linearizes the nonlinear residual and solves the resulting linear least-squares problem exactly as before.

Summary

Newton for systems. Solve DF(x⃗k)Δx⃗k=−F(x⃗k)DF(\vec x_k)\Delta\vec x_k=-F(\vec x_k)DF(xk​)Δxk​=−F(xk​), then update x⃗k+1=x⃗k+Δx⃗k\vec x_{k+1}=\vec x_k+\Delta\vec x_kxk+1​=xk​+Δxk​.

Convergence. Guaranteed locally near a root x⃗∗\vec x^*x∗ if FFF is sufficiently smooth and DF(x⃗∗)DF(\vec x^*)DF(x∗) is invertible; the theorem is silent — and the method often unreliable — when the Jacobian is singular at the root.

Gauss–Newton. To minimize E(x⃗)=12∥r⃗(x⃗)∥22E(\vec x)=\frac12\|\vec r(\vec x)\|_2^2E(x)=21​∥r(x)∥22​, solve approximately JkTJk p⃗k=−JkTr⃗(x⃗k)J_k^TJ_k\,\vec p_k=-J_k^T\vec r(\vec x_k)JkT​Jk​p​k​=−JkT​r(xk​), then update x⃗k+1=x⃗k+p⃗k\vec x_{k+1}=\vec x_k+\vec p_kxk+1​=xk​+p​k​.

Closing: Where the Two Halves of This Course Meet

It’s worth stepping back to see what just happened, because it closes a loop that has been running under this entire course. The material began with strictly linear tools — projectors, reflectors, and rotators built to introduce zeros; QR and SVD factorizations built to solve Ax=bAx=bAx=b and min⁡∥Ax−b∥2\min\|Ax-b\|_2min∥Ax−b∥2​ as stably as possible; eigenvalue and Schur decompositions built to understand what a fixed linear map does. Everything in that first half assumed the problem was linear from the start.

This chapter, and the one before it, took the opposite starting point — a genuinely nonlinear problem, F(x⃗)=0F(\vec x)=0F(x)=0 or min⁡∥r⃗(x⃗)∥22\min\|\vec r(\vec x)\|_2^2min∥r(x)∥22​ — and showed that the entire linear toolkit comes right back into play, one tangent-plane at a time. Newton’s method doesn’t avoid linear algebra when the problem stops being linear; it leans on it harder, solving a fresh linear system DF(x⃗k)Δx⃗=−F(x⃗k)DF(\vec x_k)\Delta\vec x=-F(\vec x_k)DF(xk​)Δx=−F(xk​) at every single iteration. The warning against explicitly inverting the Jacobian is the same warning against explicitly inverting ATAA^TAATA from the least-squares chapters, restated in a moving target. And Gauss–Newton, the capstone of this unit, is nothing but the normal equations reappearing exactly where a course on linear algebra might least expect them: at the heart of an algorithm for problems that are, by definition, no longer linear.

The practical upshot is a genuine unification, not just an analogy: solving a nonlinear system or fitting a nonlinear model is, at every step, a linear algebra problem — the same Ax=bAx=bAx=b and least-squares machinery built up from the very first chapter, called again and again, each time on a slightly better linear approximation of the true nonlinear world.

← Previous Solving Nonlinear Equations: Newton's Method in One and Many Dimensions Next → Finite and Infinite Sets

On this page
